candidates.name: Immersive Drill
candidates.summary: Place learners inside a simulated environment — VR headset, realistic scenario, high-stakes stress — so they build muscle memory before touching live systems. Works especially well for roles where a mistake is costly or embarrassing.
mechanics: Define 3–5 scenario archetypes that match real job moments (e.g. handling a customer dispute, operating a new tool under time pressure).
Deploy headsets or browser-based simulation to every learner site — no trainer travel needed.
Learner completes the simulation alone or in a small cohort; the system scores their choices in real time.
Debrief 15 minutes post-session to surface what surprised learners.
Track pass/fail rate as the Threshold metric; repeat until 90%+ pass.
proof: Walmart: VR cut Pickup Tower training time from 8 hours to 15 minutes (96% reduction) without sacrificing proficiency.
70% of VR-trained Walmart associates outperformed non-VR peers on post-training assessments; 10–15% higher scores.
Employee satisfaction up 30% in Walmart's VR cohort vs. control.

known_uses.name: Walmart VR Training — Strivr Platform
known_uses.quote: Associates completed training in just 15 minutes, without sacrificing training effectiveness or employee proficiency
known_uses.note: 2.2 million associates across 4,700+ locations; 60+ immersive experiences. Andy Trainor, VP of US Learning: 'Now we just send a pair of VR goggles' instead of flying three to four trainers per location.

known_uses.name: ArborXR Walmart Blueprint
known_uses.quote: Walmart rolled out over 17,000 headsets across 4,600 stores, transforming how over one million associates were trained
known_uses.note: Secondary reporting on scale and headset rollout strategy.

sources.title: Strivr helps Walmart reduce training time by 96%
sources.quote: Associates completed training in just 15 minutes, without sacrificing training effectiveness or employee proficiency

sources.title: Customer Story: Walmart's Blueprint for Training 1M+ Employees in VR
sources.quote: Walmart rolled out over 17,000 headsets across 4,600 stores, transforming how over one million associates were trained

sources.quote: Associates who completed training in VR outperformed non-VR learners on post-training skills assessments 70% of the time and achieved 10-15% higher scores

candidates.name: Earn Your Marks
candidates.summary: Layer points, badges, streaks, and tiered status onto AI learning to sustain motivation across months. Works when badge progression maps to real skill milestones — and fails when it is a thin trophy layer on top of unchanged content (the 'BPL anti-pattern').
mechanics: Design three or more skill tiers (e.g. Champion → Innovator → Legend) each unlocked only by completing verified challenges, not just consuming content.
Surface status publicly on a learner profile so social recognition reinforces continuation.
Attach each tier to a concrete capability: Champion = can build first agent; Legend = can design org-wide agent strategy.
Use streaks for daily-habit formation only — not as a substitute for skill assessment.

Anti-pattern: awarding badges purely for activity (views, clicks) without testing application; research calls this 'shallow gamification' and shows it can crowd out intrinsic motivation via the overjustification effect.
proof: Salesforce Agentblazer: Champion, Innovator, Legend tiers map to specific Agentforce capabilities; 1 million Agentblazers targeted in 2025.
Salesforce offered Agentforce Specialist Certification Exam free through 2025 to accelerate badge uptake.
Industry: gamification can boost engagement by 85% and retention by up to 50% vs. traditional methods — but only when design goes beyond the BPL triad (badges, points, leaderboards).

known_uses.name: Salesforce Agentblazer Status — Champion, Innovator, Legend
known_uses.quote: Completing these earns you badges and points, which contribute towards your Agentblazer Status
known_uses.note: Three-tier progression: Champion (AI + Agentforce fundamentals), Innovator (build custom agents), Legend (advanced org-wide strategy). Status renews annually; prior years remain visible on Trailblazer Profile.

known_uses.name: How Salesforce Plans to Train 1 Million Agentblazers
known_uses.quote: Salesforce plans to train 1 million Agentblazers for AI
known_uses.note: Goal announced 2025; free Agentforce Specialist Certification exam offered to drive adoption.

sources.quote: Completing these earns you badges and points, which contribute towards your Agentblazer Status

sources.quote: Simplistic and surficial application of game elements on a learning system or activity without transforming the core experience … overreliance on narrow models and shallow design (focusing solely on badges, points, and leaderboards) contributes to the failure of some gamification implementations.

candidates.name: Chartered Qualification
candidates.summary: Use an external, accredited certificate as the official Threshold for a Step — so learners earn a credential that is recognised beyond the employer and the training is independently verified. EU AI Act compliance pressure has made this especially salient for German-speaking organizations.
mechanics: Select a body (TÜV, IHK, Bitkom, Microsoft, AWS) whose curriculum scope matches the target Step.
Structure internal prep content to align with the cert syllabus so internal and external learning reinforce each other.
Require the cert pass (not just enrollment) as the Threshold gate for role advancement or project sign-off.
Offer exam cost reimbursement on pass to shift financial risk to the org, not the learner.
Track cert completion rates as the Masterpiece KPI for this Move.
proof: TÜV KI-Manager (TÜV): 4-day program covering AI application across business domains; dual-module structure; validated by TÜV Rheinland as IHK-partner qualification.
Bitkom Akademie KI-Manager: 8-day hybrid program co-developed with DFKI; Masterpiece is a KI Startup Pitch on the final day.
Microsoft AI-900 (Azure AI Fundamentals) and AI-902 (successor from March 2026): zero-prerequisite entry cert explicitly designed as a foundation threshold for enterprise AI roles.
EU AI Act (in force Feb 2025) mandates AI literacy for staff handling regulated AI systems — charter certs are being adopted as the compliance evidence.

candidates.name: AI-Tailored Path
candidates.summary: An AI tool analyses each learner's role, current skill level, and learning pace, then generates a personalised curriculum — so a warehouse manager and a software engineer both get to the same Threshold via different routes and at different speeds.
mechanics: Connect the platform to HR/role data so it can infer current skill level and target role.
AI engine recommends next modules drawn from a shared library (fundamentals through advanced); learner confirms or adjusts.
Platform tracks completion and adjusts future recommendations based on assessment performance.
Managers set a minimum annual learning quota (IBM uses 40 h/yr as the floor; 80 h/yr is the observed average).
Skill-gap analysis from the AI feeds directly into team upskilling plans.
proof: IBM: employees complete 80 h/yr on average against a 40 h/yr target; over 160,000 participated in IBM's watsonx challenge 2025.
IBM 'Your Learning Platform': AI suggests role-tailored modules from fundamentals through Data Scientist tracks; employees choose the most appropriate learning tailored to their role.
Gartner prediction (cited 2025): AI will personalize 80% of corporate learning content by 2025.

known_uses.name: IBM Your Learning Platform
known_uses.quote: Employees have access to numerous modules on AI via the IBM 'Your Learning Platform' … encourages them to complete at least 40 hours of learning per year
known_uses.note: Platform uses AI to recommend role-specific modules. Employees average 80 h/yr. 160,000+ participated in watsonx challenge 2025. Source: EU Digital Strategy AI Literacy Practices page.

candidates.name: Learn in the Flow
aliases: learning in the flow of work
candidates.summary: Deliver short AI skill nudges (under 10 minutes) directly inside the tools people already use — Slack, Teams, a CRM, an LMS overlay — so learning happens at the moment of need rather than during time set aside for training.
mechanics: Identify 3–5 high-frequency work moments where an AI skill nudge is useful (e.g. writing a prompt, reviewing an AI draft, escaping a hallucination).
Create micro-lessons of 2–5 minutes: one concept, one example, one practice prompt.
Embed delivery into the tool: a Teams bot, a Slack channel, a Copilot sidebar tip, or an LMS overlay triggered by job activity.
Reinforce with spaced repetition: resurface the same concept 3, 7, and 21 days later.
Track completion rate and click-through depth as lightweight Masterpiece proxies.
proof: Microlearning boosts knowledge retention by up to 50% compared to traditional methods (infoprolearning.com, 2025).
Employee engagement increases by 85% with AI-powered microlearning vs. conventional formats.
Global microlearning market: $1.55B in 2024, projected $2.96B in 2025 (CAGR 13.5%).
LinkedIn Workplace Learning Report 2025: 91% of L&D professionals say continuous learning is more important than ever.

known_uses.name: 5Mins.ai Microlearning Platform
known_uses.quote: leverages AI to deliver personalized content from its library of 20,000+ micro-lessons by 200+ industry experts based on individual roles and career goals
known_uses.note: In-flow delivery via integration with productivity tools; AI tailors which micro-lessons surface for each user.

known_uses.name: MaxLearn: Learning in the Flow of Work
known_uses.quote: LFW is designed to provide employees with relevant, timely learning experiences directly within their operational environment, integrating learning into the actual work process rather than pulling employees away from their tasks
known_uses.note: Describes architectural pattern for embedding learning triggers into CRM, collaboration tools, and performance dashboards.

candidates.name: Proof by Minutes
candidates.summary: Measure how much time per employee is spent actively using AI tools each week — the 'Copilot assisted hours' or 'weekly AI minutes' metric — and use it as the primary Masterpiece KPI for an AI adoption program.
mechanics: Enable usage telemetry in the AI platform (e.g. Microsoft Viva Insights, Worklytics, or a custom dashboard).
Set adoption tiers: low = 30–60 min/week, moderate = 2–4 h/week, high = 6+ h/week (Worklytics 2025 benchmarks).
Report per-employee and per-team averages to managers weekly; highlight novice groups for intervention.
Gate internal program Thresholds on reaching the 'habitual user' tier (≥1 active day/week for 9 of 12 weeks in Microsoft's definition).
Combine with qualitative proof (Masterpiece artifact, peer demo) so minutes alone do not become a gaming target.
proof: Worklytics 2025: 'Weekly Copilot Minutes (Active Usage Time)' listed as one of 5 essential AI adoption KPIs for managers.
Microsoft Viva Insights 'Copilot assisted hours': an estimate of total time employees were assisted by Copilot over 28 days, used in enterprise adoption dashboards from 2025.
Benchmark: enterprise users report saving 40–60 minutes per day (Vodafone/Lumen via Microsoft).
Microsoft Copilot Dashboard classifies users as power (≥15 actions/week for ≥9/12 weeks), habitual, novice, or non-user — enabling targeted re-training interventions.

known_uses.name: Worklytics: 5 New KPIs for the 2025 Manager Scorecard
known_uses.quote: Weekly Copilot Minutes (Active Usage Time) … provides concrete data on engagement depth, with organizations seeing wide variation from power users logging hours weekly to casual users spending mere minutes
known_uses.note: Benchmarks: low=30–60 min/wk; moderate=2–4 h/wk; high=6+ h/wk.

known_uses.name: Microsoft 365 Copilot Adoption Report — Viva Insights
known_uses.quote: Power user — Someone who, on average, performs 15 or more Copilot actions per week and has used Copilot in at least 9 of the past 12 weeks
known_uses.note: Dashboard segments users into power/habitual/novice/non-user; 'Copilot assisted hours' metric is the time-based aggregate for the impact report.

candidates.name: Safe Sandbox
candidates.summary: Give learners a walled environment with anonymised or synthetic data — and a clear governance layer underneath — so they can experiment boldly with AI without risk to real customer data, live systems, or compliance obligations.
mechanics: Provision a separate tenant, workspace, or environment that is air-gapped from production data.
Populate with realistic but anonymised or synthetic datasets so exercises feel authentic.
Define an acceptable-use policy for the sandbox; display it at login.
Log all activity for audit; make it visible to governance owners but anonymous to peers.
Retire the sandbox environment after a defined period so learners move to real-world use with supervision.
proof: Writer 2025: 'Writer's AI HQ acts as a sandboxed environment for experimentation that empowers employees to innovate … while the underlying governance framework ensures that all activities remain secure, compliant, and aligned with company policy.'
Writer 2025 report: 35% of employees pay out-of-pocket for AI tools they use at work — safe sandbox with sanctioned tooling directly addresses shadow-AI risk.
Delaware AI Sandbox (2025): state-level supervised environment for testing AI in corporate governance, biotechnology, healthcare sectors.
Singapore Global AI Assurance Sandbox (July 2025): specifically targets agentic AI risks including data leakage and prompt injection.

known_uses.quote: Writer's AI HQ acts as a sandboxed environment for experimentation that empowers employees to innovate and build new solutions, while the underlying governance framework ensures that all activities remain secure, compliant, and aligned with company policy
known_uses.note: Writer recommends a central AI governance group as the hub; sandbox reduces shadow AI (35% of employees pay out of pocket for unapproved AI tools).

candidates.name: Responsible Use Curriculum
candidates.summary: A structured curriculum — typically a mandatory module in every onboarding and refresh cycle — that covers the org's AI policy, acceptable use rules, bias and fairness basics, and the legal landscape (EU AI Act, copyright). Prevents naïve or reckless use before learners reach live tools.
mechanics: Open with the org's written AI policy — learner reads and acknowledges before proceeding.

Cover four areas: (1) acceptable-use rules and what violates them, (2) bias, fairness, and hallucination risks, (3) data handling (what must not go into public LLMs), (4) legal overview: EU AI Act obligations + copyright basics.
Run a scenario quiz with realistic edge cases; require ≥80% to pass.
Refresh annually — the EU AI Act and model capabilities change; curriculum must have a built-in adaptation mechanism.
Make it non-skippable as a Threshold for any AI tool provisioning.
proof: EU AI Act (in force Feb 2025): requires AI literacy training for all staff who handle AI systems in regulated contexts — responsible-use curriculum is the direct delivery vehicle.

JISC UK AI Literacy Curriculum (2025): 'Curriculum for educational and corporate contexts provides structured support to help staff develop the knowledge, practical skills and critical awareness needed to use AI effectively, responsibly and with confidence. AI literacy cannot be treated as a fixed curriculum — training must be designed with built-in mechanisms for adaptation.'
WEF Jan 2025: 'AI literacy equips individuals and organizations with the knowledge and skills needed to use AI safely, transparently and responsibly.'

known_uses.name: JISC AI Literacy Curriculum for Teaching and Learning Staff
known_uses.quote: provides structured support to help staff develop the knowledge, practical skills and critical awareness needed to use AI effectively, responsibly and with confidence
known_uses.note: July 2025 launch. Explicitly designed with adaptation mechanisms because the AI literacy landscape changes quickly.

known_uses.name: WEF: Building a Responsible AI-Ready Workforce
known_uses.quote: AI literacy equips individuals and organizations with the knowledge and skills needed to use AI safely, transparently and responsibly
known_uses.note: Jan 2025 publication; frames responsible use as a workforce literacy requirement, not optional.

candidates.name: Security Awareness for AI
candidates.summary: Extend classic security-awareness training to cover the new AI-specific threat surface: AI-generated phishing, deepfake voice/video, prompt injection into workplace AI tools, and data leakage through public LLMs. Run as a separate track from generic IT security because the threat model is fundamentally different.
mechanics: Run simulated AI-phishing exercises (AI-written emails, synthetic voice clips) so learners experience the real threat before it hits.
Teach the 'prompt injection' concept with a hands-on demo: show how a malicious document can hijack a Copilot or agent session.
Cover what must never enter a public LLM: PII, trade secrets, unpublished financials.
Deliver in short modules (10–15 min) every quarter — not as an annual compliance dump.
Measure success by click rate on simulated AI-phishing campaigns, targeting <5% post-training.
proof: AI-generated attacks went from 31% less effective than human attacks in 2023 to 24% more effective by early 2025 (Brightside AI, 100+ studies).
Deepfake incidents increased 3,000% between 2023 and early 2025; voice phishing attacks surged 400%+ year-over-year.
Orgs with comprehensive training programs reduce employee susceptibility to phishing by up to 86% from baseline.
Continuous adaptive simulation with immediate feedback outperforms annual compliance exercises.

known_uses.name: Brightside AI: Train Employees Against AI Phishing 2025
known_uses.quote: AI-generated attacks have evolved from being 31% less effective than human-crafted attacks in 2023 to 24% more effective by early 2025
known_uses.note: Specific training topics: AI-generated social engineering, unsafe GenAI usage policies, prompt injection attacks, data leakage through AI tools.

known_uses.name: SentinelOne: AI Security Awareness Training — Key Concepts
known_uses.quote: Employees now face AI-generated phishing, deepfake voice cloning, prompt injection into tools they use at work, and agentic-misuse risks
known_uses.note: Covers AI-specific threat taxonomy distinct from traditional security awareness.

candidates.name: IP and Copyright Literacy
candidates.summary: Train every practitioner on the specific IP and copyright rules that apply to AI-generated content: what copyright protects (and what it does not), what counts as a meaningful human contribution to claim authorship, and how to handle training data provenance.
mechanics: Cover four areas: (1) AI-generated content is unlikely to be copyright-protected without significant human contribution; (2) using copyrighted data to train models may not always qualify as fair use (US Copyright Office 2025 report); (3) what org-specific 'meaningful contribution' rules require for content clearance; (4) how to log AI involvement for audit purposes.
Run a short decision-tree quiz: learner classifies five example outputs as protectable / unprotectable / needs-review.
Include a one-page quick reference card for the learner's most common work scenarios.
Update annually — the US Copyright Office issued a major AI-and-copyright report in 2025 and the legal landscape is still settling.
proof: US Copyright Office Third Report on AI and Copyright (2025): 'AI-generated content without significant human input probably isn't protectable under copyright'; certain unauthorized uses of copyrighted materials to train AI cannot be defended as fair use.
WEF 2025: recommends 'defining what constitutes meaningful human contribution' and addressing 'ownership and authorship in course syllabi and research agreements'.
Harvard Bok Center AI Literacy & Ethics: institutions must 'clarify expectations for AI use' around authorship and originality.

known_uses.name: US Copyright Office Third Report on AI and Copyright
known_uses.quote: AI-generated content without significant human input probably isn't protectable under copyright … certain unauthorized uses of copyrighted materials to train AI cannot be defended as fair use
known_uses.note: 2025 report; the primary legal reference point for IP literacy training in the US. Training programs that cite this source are meeting the current standard of care.

known_uses.name: Harvard Bok Center: AI Literacy & Ethics
known_uses.quote: Departments and institutions should clarify expectations for AI use by defining what constitutes 'meaningful human contribution' and address ownership and authorship
known_uses.note: Higher-education context, but the authorship framework translates directly to corporate L&D.

candidates.name: Trust via Transparency
candidates.summary: Require AI tools and agents to display their reasoning or evidence — not just their answer — so that staff learn to verify outputs rather than blindly accept them. This is an org-level guardrail, not just a UX feature: the policy that says 'agents must explain, humans must check'.
mechanics: Set a policy: any AI output used in a decision must show a source, a confidence signal, or a chain-of-thought trace that a domain expert can verify.

Train staff on the two-pathway model: transparency increases perceived effectiveness (→ trust) but can also reveal discomfort (→ distrust) — so teams need to learn what good explanations look like, not just demand them.
Include 'verify the citation' as a mandatory step in any AI-assisted workflow checklist.
Run tabletop exercises: present an AI output with no explanation vs. one with a source trace; discuss which decision the team would stake their name on.
Track downstream error rate and human-override rate as Masterpiece metrics.
proof: Yu & Li (2022, Behavioral Sciences, n=235): AI transparency increases perceived effectiveness (promotes trust) AND increases discomfort (inhibits trust) via parallel mediation — 'AI decision-making transparency (vs. non-transparency) led to higher perceived transparency, which in turn increased both effectiveness (which promoted trust) and discomfort (which inhibited trust)'. Both effects are real and must be managed.
McKinsey 2025: 'those that fail to build trust will miss the opportunity to deliver on AI's full potential for their customers and employees'.
XAI market reached $9.77B in 2025 (CAGR 20.6%); orgs with explainable AI achieve 30% higher ROI than black-box implementations.

known_uses.name: McKinsey: Building Trust in AI — The Key Role of Explainability
known_uses.quote: those that fail to build trust will miss the opportunity to deliver on AI's full potential for their customers and employees
known_uses.note: Practitioner guide from McKinsey QuantumBlack; frames explainability as a business risk issue, not just an ethics one.

known_uses.name: Yu & Li (2022): AI Decision-Making Transparency and Employees' Trust
known_uses.quote: AI decision-making transparency (vs. non-transparency) led to higher perceived transparency, which in turn increased both effectiveness (which promoted trust) and discomfort (which inhibited trust)
known_uses.note: Peer-reviewed experimental study (n=235) establishing the dual-pathway mechanism. Confirms transparency is necessary but not sufficient — staff need training to handle the discomfort pathway.

candidates.name: Cohort Course
candidates.summary: Run a time-boxed group of 10–25 learners through the same AI curriculum in sync — live sessions, shared projects, peer accountability — so social pressure and group momentum push completion rates far above self-paced equivalents.
mechanics: Recruit 10–25 learners who share a role or target Step; cohort diversity (function mix) is optional but valuable.
Schedule 4–8 weekly live sessions of 90 minutes; supplement with async content between sessions.
Each session ends with a concrete deliverable (a working prompt, a mini-tool, a use-case memo) that peers review.
Use AI to match small groups for peer-practice pairings based on role or goal similarity.
Capstone: each learner presents a Masterpiece artifact to the cohort; peers and facilitator score it against the Threshold rubric.
proof: Self-paced course completion rates as low as 3%; cohort-based courses regularly achieve 90%+ completion.
Optimal cohort size: 10–25 learners — large enough for social learning, small enough for personal accountability.
75% of McKinsey Connected Leaders Academy participants report networking as a frequently applied post-program skill.

known_uses.name: Disco Cohort-Based Course Playbook 2025
known_uses.quote: self-paced courses have completion rates as low as 3%, while cohort-based courses often have completion rates of over 90%
known_uses.note: Comprehensive practitioner guide; also notes AI can match peer groups and surface contextual discussion prompts.

known_uses.name: McKinsey Connected Leaders Academy
known_uses.quote: 75 percent of respondents across programs reporting networking as a skill they confidently and frequently applied post-program
known_uses.note: Cohort model applied to executive leadership development; design principles transfer to AI upskilling cohorts.

sources.quote: self-paced courses have completion rates as low as 3%, while cohort-based courses often have completion rates of over 90%

sources.quote: 75 percent of respondents across programs reporting networking as a skill they confidently and frequently applied post-program

candidates.name: Leadership Retreat
candidates.summary: Take a leadership team offsite for 1–3 focused days to close the AI confidence gap and align on strategy — outside the distraction of daily operations. Combines expert-led sessions with live exercises on real business problems.
mechanics: Day 1: demystify AI — structured sessions on capabilities, limits, and competitive landscape; no jargon.
Day 2: apply AI to one real strategic question from the org's own agenda using live tools.
Day 3 (optional): define the org's AI policy and identify the first two or three use cases to fund.
Keep groups small (5–12 per table) so every leader gets hands-on time; expert facilitators rotate.
Masterpiece: each participant leaves with a written AI use-case memo for their function, reviewed by peers.
proof: AI Strategy Academy: leadership AI workshops designed to help teams 'Upskill While You Align' and 'Close the AI Confidence Gap' — bespoke strategy workshops rather than generic seminars.

FutureScale 3-Day AI for Business Leaders Retreat: brings 10 high-performing leaders together for 'an immersive three-day experience designed to equip them with the knowledge, tools, and strategy to harness AI'.
Format principle: holding retreats offsite minimises distractions; 2–5 day duration is the typical range for executive leadership retreats.

known_uses.quote: an immersive three-day experience designed to equip them with the knowledge, tools, and strategy to harness AI
known_uses.note: 10 leaders per cohort; industry-specific roundtables of 5–7 executives for in-depth dialogue.

candidates.name: AI Hackathon
candidates.summary: A company sets aside a short fixed window (typically 1-2 days) in which employees from any function form teams and build working AI prototypes against real business problems, then demo them. It pays off as a fast way to turn passive AI-curiosity into hands-on capability and to surface internal champions, while shipping prototypes leadership can act on.
mechanics: Pick AI themes tied to real business problems and announce the time window
Let employees self-organize into cross-functional teams (technical and non-technical)
Provide on-demand AI labs, tooling, and roaming mentors during the build
Teams build a working prototype against their chosen theme inside the time box
Each team demos to peers/leadership; judges award across categories
Promising prototypes get a path to continued investment
proof: TCS tcsAI Hackathon 2025 drew over 281,000 employees from 58 countries, with 29% non-technical contributors and 38% Gen Z entrants — explicitly framed as upskilling.
Microsoft Global Hackathon grew from 11,550 people in its first year (2014) to 18,304 across 400 cities and 75 countries, launching shipped products like Seeing AI.

known_uses.quote: over 281,000 employees participating from 58 countries

known_uses.name: Microsoft Global Hackathon
known_uses.quote: But we had 11,550 people that first year

sources.quote: a way to co-create the future of work by democratising AI and upskilling our people

sources.title: At the largest private hackathon on the planet, Microsoft employees fire up ideas by the thousands
sources.quote: Last year alone, Hackathon drew 18,304 people, spanned 400 cities and 75 countries

candidates.name: ShipIt Day
candidates.summary: A recurring 24-hour, ship-something-overnight build event where employees drop their day job, self-organize into teams, build anything (technical or not), and present a demo exactly 24 hours later. The hard 24-hour constraint forces autonomy, scoping, and done-over-perfect — and reliably surfaces ideas that become real products.
mechanics: Announce the quarterly 24-hour window; everyone drops normal work
Each person picks anything to work on that is not their day job
Self-organize ad-hoc cross-department teams (assemble your crew)
Build for 24 hours under the explicit constraint to ship something
Produce a short demo (e.g. 3-minute presentation or demo video)
Vote/judge across categories (People's Choice, etc.); ship the winners
proof: Atlassian ShipIt now sees nearly 4,000 Atlassians participating across more than 20 cities in at least 11 countries.
Jira Service Desk — a shipped Atlassian product — originated as a ShipIt project; FedEx Day (its predecessor) produced over 500 innovation projects in seven years per Dan Pink.

known_uses.name: ShipIt Days
known_uses.quote: Every quarter, we run a 24-hour hackathon called ShipIt where we drop everything to make something awesome.

known_uses.name: FedEx Day (origin of ShipIt)
known_uses.quote: Atlassian dubbed these innovation jamborees FedEx Days because participants have to deliver something overnight.

sources.title: "24 hours of opportunity": behind the scenes of ShipIt
sources.quote: we have nearly 4,000 Atlassians participating across more than 20 cities in at least 11 countries.

sources.title: How to deliver innovation overnight (Dan Pink)
sources.quote: these one-day bursts of autonomy allow people to work on anything they want (as long as it's not part of their regular job)

candidates.name: Hack Week
candidates.summary: An organization-wide event where everyone pauses regular work for a full week to pitch, team up, and bring new ideas to life, ending in a fair/demo. The longer week (vs. a 24-hour hack day) gives room to learn a new skill or build something substantial, and non-engineers can use it to pick up coding and AI basics.
mechanics: Kick-off event opens the week and frames any theme
Employees pitch ideas and form teams across the business
Pause regular work for five days of building
Use the runway to learn new tools/skills, not just ship
Close with a fair/expo and local People's Choice awards
Surface winners into the product roadmap
proof: Spotify's Discover Weekly playlist originated as a Hack Week idea, as did the ability to exclude select playlists from your Taste Profile.
Spotify made Hack Week an official company-wide event in 2013 with a repeatable skeleton: kick-off, a week of hacking, a fair, and local People's Choice awards.

known_uses.name: Spotify Hack Week
known_uses.quote: the annual week where they put their regular work aside and collaborate on ideas that combine their passions, creativity, and skills

candidates.name: Exploration Time Allowance
candidates.summary: A standing policy that lets employees spend a fixed slice of paid time (Google's 20%, 3M's 15%) on self-chosen projects outside their core remit. Unlike a one-off hack day, it is a continuous culture layer — ideal for sustained AI exploration where capability compounds week over week — and historically a pipeline for products like AdSense and the Post-it Note.
mechanics: Sanction a recurring percentage of paid time for self-directed work
Let people choose their own project, outside their normal duties
Provide resources/teammates so exploration can actually progress
Run it continuously rather than as a single event
Surface promising side projects into the formal roadmap
Tolerate that most explorations won't ship — value the learning
proof: Google's 20% time is credited with products such as AdSense (per Wikipedia's Side project time entry).
3M reports a 15% culture dating to ~1948 that seeded the Post-it Note — but the official 3M page could not be fetched, so this leg is unverified.

known_uses.quote: 20 percent of an employee's time may be used for side projects

known_uses.quote: spend 15 percent of their working time

candidates.name: Team Capstone
candidates.summary: A cohort's training culminates in a small team building a real, sponsor-supplied AI/data project end-to-end, so the deliverable doubles as both the proof of learning and a usable business artifact. The team project is the training vehicle, not a side exercise after the real lessons.
mechanics: Source real challenges from sponsoring employers or the learners' own backlog rather than toy datasets
Form small cross-functional teams (typically 4-6) and assign each a scoped, ambitious-but-finishable problem
Run it as the final block of a multi-week cohort so prior lessons are immediately applied under pressure
Give teams a coach/mentor and checkpoints; require a working production-aligned deliverable, not slides
Hold a public demo/grand-finale where teams present to judges and the sponsoring org
Hand the best prototypes directly to engineering or the business for deployment
proof: Correlation One DS4A/Empowerment: from over 100 data and analytics projects, top teams were awarded prizes; one cohort had 1,006 Fellows over 14 weeks of training and project work.
Correlation One enterprise capstones cite a 90-day time-to-measurable-ROI and prototypes that can go directly to engineering for development.

candidates.name: AI Residency
candidates.summary: A fixed-term (typically 6-12 month) full-time position where someone from an adjacent field is embedded in a real AI team, learns by doing frontier work under senior mentorship, and converts the experience into a permanent role. It is a hiring-and-training hybrid: the residency itself is the curriculum.
mechanics: Recruit capable people from adjacent fields (maths, physics, neuroscience, software) who are not yet AI specialists
Embed them full-time inside a research/build team for a fixed term (6 months at OpenAI, 12 months at Meta/Google)
Pair each resident with senior researcher/engineer mentors who co-pick a real problem of mutual interest
Have residents design and run real experiments / ship real techniques, not coursework
Communicate outputs to the community (papers, internal launches) as proof of capability
Extend full-time offers to residents who perform well at the end of the term
proof: OpenAI Residency is a six-month program and a pathway to a full-time role for researchers and engineers who don't currently focus on AI.
Meta AI Residency is a one-year research training position giving hands-on experience with AI research on a problem of mutual interest.

known_uses.name: OpenAI Residency
known_uses.quote: Residents join OpenAI as full-time employees, embedding within Research teams to work on cutting-edge problems under the mentorship of senior researchers and engineers.

candidates.name: AI Apprenticeship & Shadowing
candidates.summary: A learner is placed alongside an expert and does real AI work under close supervision over an extended period (months to years), with structured off-the-job study woven around live project contributions. Learning comes primarily from supervised real work, not classroom delivery.
mechanics: Place the apprentice inside a real team on live organisational AI/data work, not a sandbox
Assign a dedicated mentor/coach plus a workplace supervisor for day-to-day guidance
Ring-fence dedicated learning time (e.g. ~20% off-the-job) for structured study alongside the work
Have the learner contribute under close supervision, deliberately trading speed for learning
Build a portfolio of real deliverables that evidences competence over a long horizon (15 months to multi-year)
Convert successful apprentices into permanent/entry roles
proof: Cambridge Spark's L6 AI Engineer Apprenticeship runs 15 months with a dedicated Digital Mentor and Learner Success Coach and defined off-the-job training time.
LinkedIn REACH allocates 20% of time for self-development, runs one to five years, and has apprentices own real business-need projects.

sources.title: The LunarTech Apprenticeship Programs

candidates.name: Action Learning Set
candidates.summary: A small standing group (a set) meets regularly; each member brings a real, unsolved problem (e.g. an AI adoption or automation challenge), gets uninterrupted airtime, and the others advance it through questioning rather than advice, followed by reflection and committed action before the next meeting. Revans captured it as L = P + Q: programmed knowledge plus questioning.
mechanics: Form a small set of peers (typically 5-8) who are each working on a genuine, current problem
Give one member at a time uninterrupted time to present their challenge
The rest help mainly by asking open, probing questions rather than dispensing solutions
The presenter commits to concrete actions before the next meeting (no learning without action)
Members return, report what happened, and reflect on results to extract learning
A facilitator holds the process (or the set self-facilitates once mature)
proof: Revans' principle: there can be no learning without action, and no action without learning.
Organisations adopting action-learning — Oxfam, Heineken, EY — report improved cross-functional working, ownership and progress against objectives.

known_uses.name: Action Learning at Oxfam, Heineken and EY
known_uses.quote: Organisations adopting action-learning, such as Oxfam, Heineken and EY, report a whole host of additional benefits such as improved cross-functional working, greater ownership, emotional intelligence and progress against objectives.

known_uses.name: Action Learning method (Reg Revans, originator)
known_uses.quote: There can be no learning without action, and no action without learning

sources.title: The missing link in L&D: Why action-learning deserves a comeback
sources.quote: peer groups get together in what is known as an 'action-learning set'; one person has uninterrupted time to share their challenge.

sources.title: Action learning - Wikipedia
sources.quote: An approach to problem solving that involves taking action and reflecting upon the results.

candidates.name: Lunch & Learn Drip
candidates.summary: A short, recurring, low-pressure session (often over lunch) where someone presents one AI topic, tool, or use case followed by Q&A. Keeps a whole org learning in small, regular bites instead of one big training event.
mechanics: Run on a fixed light cadence (monthly, or weekly bite-sized slots)
One presenter takes ~20 min on a single AI aspect or tool
Leave ~15-20 min for questions and discussion
Keep it informal and voluntary; pick lighter topics so it doesn't feel like work
Rotate presenters and let attendee questions shape the next topics
proof: Minitex ran AI Knowledge Bytes as a standing 40-minute Tuesday Lunch 'n Learn series across libraries.
2i2c runs monthly Brown Bag tech talks as informal 45-minute knowledge-sharing sessions.

known_uses.name: AI Knowledge Bytes: AI in Libraries Lunch 'n Learn Series
known_uses.quote: Sessions will consist of a 20 minute presentation on a specific aspect of AI in Libraries, with 15-20 minutes left for questions and comments.

known_uses.name: Brown Bag Tech Talks
known_uses.quote: A 'Brown Bag' session is intended to be an informal meeting or training session.

candidates.name: Mob with AI
candidates.summary: The whole team works one screen at a time WITH an AI tool in the loop, so everyone learns to prompt, critique, and refine AI outputs together. Spreads AI skill evenly and stops a single AI power-user silo from forming.
mechanics: Whole team works together on one task at one machine, rotating driver/navigator roles
Bring an AI coding assistant into the mob as a shared collaborator
Everyone prompts, critiques, and refines the AI output live as a group
Review as you build instead of separate code reviews
Run it as a time-boxed experiment (e.g. a couple of months) and measure cycle time
proof: Atlassian's Micros Extensibility team merged most PRs within a day or two vs ~a week under the usual workflow.
AI drafts reached ~80% of target, needing only one or two small adjustments before merge.

known_uses.name: Mobbing with AI (Micros Extensibility team)
known_uses.quote: By using AI in a mob setting, the entire team learns how to prompt, critique, and refine AI outputs. This prevents the 'AI power user' silo and spreads skills evenly.

known_uses.name: Mob Programming with AI
known_uses.quote: We use AI like a superintelligent advisor—always available, never tired.

candidates.name: AI Demo Day
candidates.summary: Teams spend a focused block learning-by-doing with AI, then gather to demo and show-and-tell what they built and learned. The live, relatable demo by a peer or leader is a proven catalyst for AI adoption.
mechanics: Set aside a dedicated block (a day or a few hours) for hands-on AI work
Form small groups and pick real use cases your teammates actually face
Experiment and build with AI tools
Gather at the end so teams demo and share what they learned and the challenges they hit
Capture and circulate the best demos to inspire wider adoption
proof: Rovo usage increased 90% among those who saw an Atlassian leader demo one of their own AI use cases.
AI Innovation Day runs 2-4 hours with 3-11 people and explicitly ends in demos/show-and-tell.

known_uses.name: AI Innovation Day (play)

candidates.name: AI Lightning Talks
candidates.summary: A series of very short (3-15 min) peer talks on AI use cases, tools, and lessons, run on a light recurring cadence. The low prep bar keeps a steady flow of shared, actionable AI knowledge across a team or function; the Japanese LT会 meetup is the same format at community scale.
mechanics: Keep each talk short (3-5 min, up to ~15) with a few slides; lower the bar so people don't over-polish
Open a sign-up for short slots; anything AI-related is fair game
Run talks back-to-back; the strict time box forces one clear point per talk
Build in networking time so attendees connect over what was shown
Run monthly to keep momentum; rotate presenters and welcome first-timers
proof: University of Iowa OneIT ran AI Lightning Talks as a documented monthly series (Jan 2025 through June 2026).
Recurring AI-focused LT会 series run in Japan (e.g. AIフレンズ), explicitly designed for low-stakes, beginner-friendly sharing.

known_uses.name: AI Lightning Talks
known_uses.quote: AI Lightning Talks are events that feature short 3-5 minute talks covering topics such as AI Use cases, projects, concepts, or impacts.

candidates.name: AI Book Club
candidates.summary: A recurring cohort reads an AI book (or papers) and meets to discuss it, building shared AI literacy and judgment through conversation rather than a course. A common entry book is Ethan Mollick's Co-Intelligence.
mechanics: Pick an AI book or paper set and a fixed cadence (e.g. a chapter per week, or one book per month)
Meet on a standing schedule (weekly Zoom over several weeks, or monthly)
Run interactive, discussion-based sessions rather than lectures
Tie discussion to how AI affects participants' actual work and judgment
Provide a facilitator/coordinator and a reading schedule
proof: Austin Community College's FCLI ran a multi-week Co-Intelligence club open to all faculty (weekly, spring 2026).
The I'd Rather Be Writing AI Book Club sustains a one-book-a-month cadence.

candidates.name: Silent Co-Working Study Session
candidates.summary: A scheduled block where people gather (in person or online) to work quietly and individually on their own AI/LLM projects at their own pace, with the room available for peer questions between bursts of focused work. The Japanese mokumoku (silent/absorbed) format turns the social pull of a meetup into protected, distraction-free build time rather than passive lecture-watching.
mechanics: Organizer books a venue or online room for a fixed window (often 2-4 hours) and posts a topic (e.g. 生成AI x Agent SDK)
Each participant brings their own task — coding, an LLM experiment, image generation, writing — and works on it silently
No agenda or speaker; the default mode is heads-down individual work
Lightweight peer exchange (chat, voice, brief intros) is allowed between focus periods so people can unblock each other
Often a short share-out at the end where people say what they got done
proof: Recurring connpass series run topic-specific generative-AI sessions (e.g. 京橋もくもく組 running 生成AI x Agent SDK).
Free or low-cost, beginner-friendly framing lowers the barrier to starting a real build; numbered recurring events indicate a sustained format.

candidates.name: Rotating Book-Reading Circle
candidates.summary: A recurring group works through one technical book (or paper set) together, with members taking turns presenting a chapter and the group discussing it before moving on. The rotating-presenter duty forces each member to read deeply enough to teach, which is how a 輪読会 converts a hard AI/ML book into shared, durable team understanding.
mechanics: Group picks one book (e.g. an ML/deep-learning title or a generative-AI book) and a fixed cadence
Presenter duty rotates (持ち回り式): each session one member presents the assigned chapter
The chapter is discussed openly rather than just lectured, surfacing questions and misunderstandings
The group advances one chapter/section per session until the book is finished, then picks the next
Scales from beginner books to frontier-paper reading (used by university labs)
proof: Long-running connpass groups read AI/ML books one volume at a time (機械学習プロフェッショナルシリーズ輪読会, 383 members).
Generative-AI book circles (コード×AI 実践勉強会) extend the format to current LLM/agent practice.

candidates.name: Barcamp Unconference
candidates.summary: A participant-driven unconference with no fixed agenda: attendees propose session topics on the morning of the event, the room votes, and a grid of self-run sessions (discussions, short impulses, hands-on workshops) is filled live. For AI enablement this means the program tracks exactly what the community needs to learn that day, with everyone an equal contributor rather than audience.
mechanics: Open call brings together a mixed crowd (business, science, education, civil society)
Morning session-planning slot: anyone pitches a topic they want to run or discuss
Participants vote on interest; popular topics are slotted into the time/room grid
Sessions run as open formats — discussion, short impulse talk, or practical workshop
Egalitarian rule: everyone may ask, share an idea, or host a session
proof: Recurring German KI-Barcamps build their program live (KI Barcamp Jena; HNEE Eberswalde barcamp, ~120 attendees, 17 sessions).
Session topics emerge from participants on the day (e.g. Doom-Prompting, Tool-Souveränität), contrasting deliberately with lecture formats.

known_uses.name: KI Barcamp in Jena (weil KI uns alle betrifft)
known_uses.quote: Ein Barcamp ist eine Unkonferenz: kein fertiges Programm, sondern Themen, die von den Teilnehmenden selbst eingebracht werden. Alle sind gleichberechtigt.

known_uses.name: Barcamp 'KI für eine bessere Welt?!'
known_uses.quote: Rund 120 Unternehmer:innen, Studierende und Menschen aus Wissenschaft, Zivilgesellschaft und Verwaltung trafen sich am 18. September 2025 ... zu einem Barcamp über den verantwortungsvollen Einsatz von KI.

sources.title: KI Barcamp in Jena – weil KI uns alle betrifft
sources.quote: Statt starrer Vorträge gibt es offene Sessions – von Diskussionen über kurze Impulse bis zu praktischen Workshops ist alles möglich.

sources.title: KI verantwortungsvoll einsetzen – Das war unser Barcamp
sources.quote: Rund 120 Unternehmer:innen, Studierende und Menschen aus Wissenschaft, Zivilgesellschaft und Verwaltung trafen sich ... zu einem Barcamp über den verantwortungsvollen Einsatz von KI.

candidates.name: AI Regulars' Table
candidates.summary: A low-formality, recurring (often monthly) get-together — the German Stammtisch regulars'-table tradition applied to AI — where companies, practitioners and curious newcomers swap current needs, experiences, and what they've actually solved with AI. It is a peer-exchange/acculturation Move: no curriculum, just a dependable rhythm that keeps a regional or organizational community learning from each other's real cases.
mechanics: A host (digital hub, university, chamber, library, or company) sets a fixed recurring slot (e.g. every 3rd Wednesday of the month)
Open, free attendance; mix of regional firms, experts, and students/newcomers
Each meeting often has a light theme but the core is open exchange of current AI needs and experiences
Participants share which problems are already being solved with AI and trade practical lessons
The recurring cadence builds a stable regional/peer network over time rather than one-off contact
proof: Many parallel German-speaking series run monthly (Mittelstand-Digital Zentrum Magdeburg; Handwerk online, 3rd Wed monthly).
Explicitly framed as exchange of needs and developments, bridging companies and universities/experts.

known_uses.name: KI-Stammtisch
known_uses.quote: Ziel des Treffens ist der Austausch zu aktuellen Bedarfen und Entwicklungen im Bereich der künstlichen Intelligenz (KI). Tauschen Sie sich mit Unternehmen und Fachexperten zu Erfahrungen im Einsatz von KI aus.

sources.title: KI-Stammtisch - Mittelstand-Digital Zentrum Magdeburg
sources.quote: Ziel des Treffens ist der Austausch zu aktuellen Bedarfen und Entwicklungen im Bereich der künstlichen Intelligenz (KI)

sources.title: Monatlicher Online-Stammtisch: Künstliche Intelligenz in Mittelstand und Handwerk
sources.quote: Der Online-KI-Stammtisch findet jeden 3. Mittwoch im Monat statt.

candidates.name: Self-Organized Study Cohort
candidates.summary: A peer-run, time-boxed study cohort (the Korean 스터디) where members commit to a weekly rhythm of finding, summarizing, and presenting real AI use cases to each other — accountability and peer teaching, not an instructor lecturing. It differs from a bootcamp: there is no formal teacher, the curriculum is the members' own curated cases, and the engine is weekly public commitment (인증) within a fixed cohort (기수).
mechanics: A community opens a numbered cohort (기수) for a fixed run (often ~4-8 weeks) with a set theme
Each week every member finds a few interesting AI use-case posts from the community database
Members summarize each case in a few sentences and submit it — weekly proof-of-work (인증)
Submissions are turned into presentation slides and members present their most striking case to the cohort
Complete beginners are welcomed; learning is hands-on and shared rather than lecture-only
proof: 지피터스 (GPTers) runs cohort after cohort (23기 recruiting), validated by thousands of shared use cases.
The weekly find/summarize/present loop and open admission of total beginners are documented on the study page.

known_uses.name: 지피터스 AI 활용사례 탐색 스터디
known_uses.quote: 매주 본인이 직접 지피터스 게시판에서 흥미로운 게시글 3개를 찾아봅니다. 찾은 게시글 각각의 흥미로운 포인트를 3-4문장으로 요약해서 제출합니다. AI 완전 입문자도 참여 가능합니다!

sources.title: 지피터스 AI 활용사례 탐색 스터디
sources.quote: 제출한 내용과 게시글 내용을 바탕으로 발표용 슬라이드 초안을 만들어드립니다!

sources.title: 지피터스 AI 스터디 23기 대기자 모집중
sources.quote: 📣 23기 AI 스터디 대기자 모집 중!
